name: Arawn
description: Arawn, a significant figure in Welsh mythology, particularly within the Mabinogion, reigns as the king of Annwn, the Otherworld. Unlike many depictions of underworld rulers in other mythologies, Arawn is not inherently malevolent. His portrayal is complex, exhibiting both regal power and a capacity for honorable dealings. He is associated with the hunt, war, and the realm of the dead, representing a potent combination of forces that shape the mortal world. His court is described as a place of both beauty and terror, reflecting the duality inherent in the concept of the Otherworld. Arawn's interactions with mortal heroes, such as Pwyll, highlight the permeable boundaries between the human and supernatural realms, and the potential for both conflict and alliance. He demonstrates power over the landscape and its creatures, as well as the ability to shift between realms, further solidifying his position as a powerful and enigmatic figure in the Welsh pantheon. He commands spectral hounds and possesses a wealth of magical power.
subtitle: King of Annwn

keyMyths.title: Pwyll Prince of Dyfed and Arawn
keyMyths.description: Pwyll, Prince of Dyfed, while hunting, stumbles upon Arawn's hounds devouring a stag. He drives the hounds away and sets his own upon the kill, angering Arawn. To atone for his offense, Pwyll agrees to exchange places with Arawn for a year and a day. During this time, Pwyll must rule Annwn with Arawn's appearance and prevent any harm from befalling the kingdom. He succeeds, even resisting the advances of Arawn's wife, demonstrating his honor and integrity. This exchange highlights the interconnectedness of the mortal and Otherworldly realms and the importance of upholding one's word.

keyMyths.title: The Battle Against Hafgan
keyMyths.description: As part of his agreement with Arawn, Pwyll is tasked with defeating Hafgan, Arawn's rival. Hafgan can only be killed with a single blow, a vulnerability Pwyll exploits during their battle. With a single, decisive strike, Pwyll vanquishes Hafgan, bringing peace to Annwn and solidifying Arawn's rule. This act demonstrates Pwyll's prowess as a warrior and his loyalty to Arawn, further strengthening their bond. The battle serves as a crucial event in Pwyll's heroic journey and establishes Arawn as a figure worthy of respect and alliance.

keyMyths.title: The Return to Dyfed
keyMyths.description: After a year and a day, Pwyll and Arawn resume their original forms and return to their respective kingdoms. Pwyll finds that his realm has prospered during his absence, and Arawn is grateful for Pwyll's service. The bond forged between them lasts a lifetime, establishing a precedent for cooperation between the mortal and Otherworldly realms. This event underscores the importance of honor, loyalty, and the potential for mutually beneficial relationships between different worlds. The tale serves as a testament to the power of integrity and the rewards that come from upholding one's commitments.

sources.author: Gwyn Jones and Thomas Jones
